source: Conan O'Brien - The Kennedy Center Mark Twain Prize for American Humor (2025)
description: Sophisticated vocabulary showcasing Conan's Harvard Lampoon background - literary references, archaic constructions, philosophical terms, and clever wordplay

words.definition: Highly praised or celebrated; acclaimed with great admiration
words.etymology: Middle English vaunten, from Old French vanter, from Latin vanitas 'emptiness, vanity'
words.context: This vaunted stage will now showcase distinguished, elegant, and revered entertainment
words.speaker: Announcer (opening)

words.definition: Devoted followers or assistants; originally referring to those who assist in religious ceremonies
words.etymology: Late Latin acolythus, from Greek akolouthos 'follower, companion'
words.context: Meanwhile, all his radicalized acolytes were just left wandering around the country, jaded and paranoid
words.speaker: John Mulaney

words.definition: The fact of two things being seen or placed close together with contrasting effect
words.etymology: Latin juxta 'next' + positio 'position'
words.context: It was this juxtaposition of decorum and chaos that made Conan so special to me
words.speaker: Nikki Glaser

words.definition: Behavior in keeping with good taste and propriety; etiquette
words.etymology: Latin decorum 'that which is proper, suitable', from decus 'ornament, honor'
words.context: It was this juxtaposition of decorum and chaos that made Conan so special
words.speaker: Nikki Glaser

words.definition: In low spirits from loss of hope or courage; in a dejected manner
words.etymology: Latin despondere 'to give up, promise', from de- 'away' + spondere 'to pledge'
words.context: A good Catholic boy despondently staring into the fake night sky
words.speaker: Nikki Glaser

words.definition: Denying any responsibility or support for something; repudiating
words.etymology: Old French desavouer, from des- 'opposite of' + avouer 'acknowledge'
words.context: Disavowing any responsibility for a bit he not only approved of, but rehearsed
words.speaker: Nikki Glaser

words.definition: Morally corrupt; wicked; characterized by depravity
words.etymology: Latin depravare 'to make crooked, corrupt', from de- 'thoroughly' + pravus 'crooked'
words.context: You also gave us permission to laugh at some of the most depraved, immoral comedy
words.speaker: Nikki Glaser

words.definition: A sharing of characteristics or origins; a feeling of being close or connected to other people
words.etymology: Old English cynn 'family, race' + -ship
words.context: I always felt a kinship for Conan, even before I met him, just as a fellow redhead
words.speaker: Bill Burr

words.definition: Wandering aimlessly; following no particular course; rambling
words.etymology: From the River Maeander in Asia Minor, known for its winding course
words.context: Telling their long, meandering stories that went nowhere
words.speaker: Bill Burr

words.definition: Persons who inspire or influence others, especially those prominent in a particular sphere
words.etymology: Latin lumen 'light', from lucere 'to shine'
words.context: Today, Conan joins such luminaries as Julia Louis-Dreyfus, Bob Newhart, Adam Sandler, and Steve Martin
words.speaker: Kumail Nanjiani

words.definition: Very great or intense; having deep insight or understanding; intellectually deep
words.etymology: Latin profundus 'deep', from pro- 'before' + fundus 'bottom'
words.context: It's a profoundly generous thing for a performer to let another performer sit next to them
words.speaker: Andy Richter

words.definition: Giving satisfaction or pleasure; affording emotional fulfillment
words.etymology: Latin gratificare 'to show favor to', from gratus 'pleasing'
words.context: It's gratifying to realize how far we've made it
words.speaker: Andy Richter

words.definition: Deep respect for someone or something; a feeling or attitude of deep respect tinged with awe
words.etymology: Latin reverentia, from revereri 'to stand in awe of'
words.context: We expect our graduates to have a reverence for well-respected citizens of the upper class
words.speaker: Henfield Horgan (Harvard sketch)

words.definition: Exaggerated statements or claims not meant to be taken literally; overstatement for effect
words.etymology: Greek hyperbole 'a throwing beyond, excess', from hyper- 'beyond' + ballein 'to throw'
words.context: And, honestly, I mean this with no hyperbole, he attended Harvard University
words.speaker: Henfield Horgan (Harvard sketch)

words.definition: People or things of very great importance, size, or power; from Greek mythology
words.etymology: Greek Titanes, the divine giants who preceded the Olympian gods
words.context: At Harvard, we aim to create titans of industry, finance, and government
words.speaker: Henfield Horgan (Harvard sketch)

words.definition: Perceptible by touch; clear and definite; real and substantial
words.etymology: Latin tangere 'to touch'
words.context: People who made a tremendous and tangible difference in the world
words.speaker: Henfield Horgan (Harvard sketch)

words.definition: Admirably purposeful, determined, and unwavering; firm in purpose
words.etymology: Latin resolutus 'loosened, dissolved', from resolvere 'to untie'
words.context: Times change, but people have to stay resolute
words.speaker: Mark Twain (Will Forte)

words.definition: Allowing light, but not detailed shapes, to pass through; semitransparent (used metaphorically for personality)
words.etymology: Latin translucere 'to shine through', from trans- 'through' + lucere 'to shine'
words.context: You're a translucent man-child who has bumbled and stumbled through 30 years
words.speaker: Mark Twain (Will Forte)

words.definition: Occurring or done in brief, irregular bursts; fitful; characterized by spasms
words.etymology: Greek spasmos 'spasm, convulsion', from span 'to draw, pull'
words.context: 30 years of meaningless spasmodic idiocy
words.speaker: Mark Twain (Will Forte)

words.definition: Lack of skill, ability, or competence; awkwardness or inappropriateness
words.etymology: Latin ineptus 'unsuitable, foolish', from in- 'not' + aptus 'fit'
words.context: Our ineptitude, and the glorious mess of being human
words.speaker: Conan O'Brien

words.definition: A shared feature or attribute; the state of sharing characteristics; solidarity
words.etymology: Latin communis 'common, shared by all'
words.context: We acknowledge our commonality, and we move just a little closer together
words.speaker: Conan O'Brien

words.definition: Extreme patriotism, especially in the form of aggressive or warlike foreign policy
words.etymology: From British music hall song 'We don't want to fight but by Jingo if we do'
words.context: Twain was suspicious of populism, jingoism, imperialism, the money-obsessed mania of the Gilded Age
words.speaker: Conan O'Brien

bonus_advanced_words.definition: A political approach that strives to appeal to ordinary people who feel that their concerns are disregarded by established elite groups
bonus_advanced_words.context: Twain was suspicious of populism, jingoism, imperialism
bonus_advanced_words.speaker: Conan O'Brien

bonus_advanced_words.definition: A policy of extending a country's power and influence through diplomacy or military force
bonus_advanced_words.context: Twain was suspicious of populism, jingoism, imperialism
bonus_advanced_words.speaker: Conan O'Brien

bonus_advanced_words.definition: Intolerance toward those who hold different opinions from oneself; obstinate belief in the superiority of one's own opinions
bonus_advanced_words.etymology: French bigot 'bigot, hypocrite', possibly from Old Spanish bigote 'mustache'
bonus_advanced_words.context: Travel is fatal to prejudice, bigotry, and narrow-mindedness
bonus_advanced_words.speaker: Conan O'Brien (quoting Twain)

notes: This collection emphasizes Conan's intellectual depth - his Harvard education shines through in philosophical terms (ineptitude, commonality), political vocabulary (jingoism, imperialism, populism), literary constructions (translucent, spasmodic), and classical references (titans, acolytes). The words span multiple speakers but particularly showcase Conan's own acceptance speech where he channels Mark Twain's intellectual legacy.
